class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Voice User Interface Design
learning_objectives: Understand the architecture of voice platforms: ASR, NLU, dialog management, TTS
Apply cooperative conversation principles from linguistics to VUI design
Identify when voice is the right modality vs. visual or multimodal
readings: Designing Voice User Interfaces by Cathy Pearl, Ch. 1-3
Conversational Design by Erika Hall, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Voice is the most natural human communication modality but the least natural computer interface. VUI design bridges this gap by applying conversation principles to technology interaction.
key_concepts: Voice platform architecture: ASR (speech-to-text), NLU (intent/entity extraction), dialog management, TTS (text-to-speech)
Grice's maxims applied to VUI: quantity, quality, relevance, manner
When voice works: hands-busy, eyes-busy, speed advantage, accessibility
When voice fails: privacy-sensitive contexts, complex visual data, noisy environments
Multimodal design: voice + screen for the best of both worlds
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deconstructing a voice interaction with Alexa — mapping the technical pipeline from user utterance through ASR, NLU, dialog management, and TTS response

review.value: Say enough to be understood but no more than necessary — voice responses should be concise because users cannot scan audio the way they scan text.
Voice excels when users' hands or eyes are occupied (cooking, driving), for quick queries, and for accessibility. It fails for complex data comparison, private information, and noisy environments.

class_schedule.topic: Dialog Flow Design and Intent Modeling
learning_objectives: Model user intents and entities for a voice application domain
Design dialog flows with branching, context management, and slot filling
Handle multi-turn conversations with context carryover
readings: Designing Voice User Interfaces by Cathy Pearl, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Dialog flows are the interaction architecture of voice applications. They define what users can say, how the system interprets it, and how conversations progress through multiple turns.
key_concepts: Intents: the user's goal (book_flight, check_weather, play_music)
Entities: the variables within an intent (destination, date, artist_name)
Slot filling: collecting required entities across conversation turns
Context management: remembering information across turns in a conversation
Fallback handling: what happens when the system cannot determine intent
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete dialog flow for a restaurant reservation bot in Voiceflow — defining intents, entities, slot filling, and multi-turn context

review.value: Collecting required information piece by piece across turns: 'I want to book a table.' 'For how many people?' 'Four.' 'What date?' 'Friday.' — each turn fills one slot until the intent can be fulfilled.
Remembering previous conversation context: after booking a table, 'Can you change the time?' refers to the reservation just made without the user re-specifying the restaurant.

learning_objectives: Design robust error handling for no-match, no-input, and disambiguation scenarios
Implement escalation strategies from reprompt through human handoff
Write error prompts that guide users toward successful interactions
readings: Designing Voice User Interfaces by Cathy Pearl, Ch. 7-8
Google Conversation Design: Error Handling guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: Voice interactions fail more often than visual interactions because users can say anything. Robust error handling is what separates usable VUIs from frustrating ones.
key_concepts: No-match errors: system cannot determine intent from user speech
No-input errors: user does not respond within the timeout window
Disambiguation: system detected multiple possible intents
Escalation ladder: first try (rephrase), second try (constrain), third try (handoff)
Graceful degradation: offering alternative modalities when voice fails
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the complete error handling flow for a banking voice bot — handling misrecognized account numbers, ambiguous transfer requests, and security verification failures

review.value: First error: gently rephrase ('Sorry, I did not catch that. What city?'). Second error: constrain options ('Would you like New York, Chicago, or Los Angeles?'). Third error: offer alternative ('Let me transfer you to an agent who can help.').

Error prompts should be shorter than original prompts — the user already has context. 'What city?' not 'I did not understand your response. Could you please tell me what city you would like to travel to?'

class_schedule.topic: VUI Persona Design and Prompt Writing
learning_objectives: Create a voice persona that aligns with brand identity and user expectations
Write prompts that are natural, concise, and guide user behavior
Apply prompt variation to prevent repetitive-sounding interactions
readings: Wired for Speech by Clifford Nass, Ch. 1-3
Amazon Alexa Design Guide: Voice Design Best Practices

lecture_notes.introduction: Every voice interface has a persona whether you design one intentionally or not. A well-designed VUI persona builds trust, sets expectations, and creates a consistent conversational experience.
key_concepts: VUI persona elements: name, personality traits, vocabulary, speech patterns
Prompt types: initial prompts, reprompts, confirmation prompts, error prompts
Conciseness rules: say it in half the words, then cut again
Prompt variation: multiple versions of the same prompt to prevent robotic repetition
Earcon design: non-speech audio cues for system state (listening, processing, error)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a complete VUI persona for a fitness coaching app — defining personality, writing all prompt types with variations, and designing earcons
setup_requirements: Audio recording setup for prompt testing

review.value: Users process audio linearly and cannot re-read. Rule of thumb: if a prompt takes more than 5 seconds to speak, it is too long. Cut filler words, remove redundancy, lead with the most important information.

Having 3-4 versions of each prompt prevents the system from sounding robotic in repeated interactions: 'Got it.', 'Done.', 'All set.', 'That is saved.' — natural conversations never repeat the same phrase identically.

learning_objectives: Conduct usability testing for voice interfaces using appropriate methods
Design multimodal experiences that combine voice with visual displays
Measure VUI quality using voice-specific metrics
readings: Designing Voice User Interfaces by Cathy Pearl, Ch. 10-11

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing voice interfaces requires different techniques than testing visual interfaces. Users cannot point at what confused them, and the interaction is ephemeral — gone as soon as the words are spoken.
key_concepts: VUI testing methods: Wizard of Oz, prototype testing, live system testing
Voice-specific metrics: intent recognition rate, task completion, dialog turns to completion
Multimodal design: what to show on screen vs. what to say
Screen as complement: showing what voice cannot efficiently convey (lists, images, maps)
Testing multimodal: ensuring voice and screen stay synchronized
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a Wizard of Oz voice test — one student plays the system while another plays the user, with the class observing and noting interaction breakdowns
setup_requirements: Wizard of Oz script and response guide

review.value: The number of conversation turns required to complete a task — fewer is better. If setting a timer takes 5 turns instead of 2, the dialog flow needs redesign.

Voice handles: commands, queries, quick responses. Screen handles: lists, comparison data, images, persistent information. Neither should duplicate the other — each modality contributes what it does best.

questions.question: Design the intent model for a pizza ordering voice bot. List at least 3 intents, their required entities, and provide 3 sample utterances for the primary ordering intent.
questions.answer: Intents: (1) order_pizza — entities: size (small/medium/large), crust (thin/regular/thick), toppings (list), quantity. (2) check_order_status — entities: order_number or phone_number. (3) cancel_order — entities: order_number. Sample utterances for order_pizza: 'I want to order a large pepperoni pizza', 'Can I get two medium pizzas with mushrooms and olives', 'Order me a thin crust pizza with extra cheese'. Training utterances should vary in structure, length, and entity placement to improve NLU recognition.

rubric.key: 3 varied sample utterances for ordering intent
Entities have appropriate types (enum, list, free text)

questions.question: Explain Grice's maxim of quantity as applied to VUI prompt writing. Give an example of a prompt that violates this maxim and rewrite it correctly.
questions.answer: Grice's maxim of quantity states: provide as much information as needed, but no more. In VUI, this means prompts should give the user exactly enough context to respond correctly — not less (which causes confusion) and not more (which wastes time and cognitive effort). Violation: 'Thank you for your interest in checking the current weather conditions. I can provide you with detailed weather information including temperature, humidity, wind speed, and precipitation forecasts for any city in the United States. Which city would you like to know about?' Correct: 'Which city do you want the weather for?' The corrected version provides just enough context (it is about weather, it needs a city) without unnecessary preamble.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of the maxim applied to VUI
Violation example clearly demonstrates the problem

questions.question: Write the complete escalation sequence (3 attempts) for a voice banking app that fails to understand the user's account number. Each attempt should use a different strategy.
questions.answer: Attempt 1 — gentle rephrase: 'I did not catch that. What is your account number?' Attempt 2 — constrain and coach: 'Please say your 10-digit account number slowly, one digit at a time.' Attempt 3 — offer alternative: 'I am having trouble with the account number. You can enter it using your phone keypad, or I can transfer you to an agent. Which would you prefer?' The escalation progresses from open retry to constrained input to modality switch — each attempt tries a different strategy rather than repeating the same approach.

rubric.key: Prompts get shorter with each attempt
Final attempt offers alternative modality or human handoff

questions.question: Why is prompt variation important in VUI design? Give an example showing 3 variations of a confirmation prompt and explain when each might be used.
questions.answer: Prompt variation prevents the system from sounding robotic during repeated interactions. Hearing the identical phrase every time breaks the illusion of conversation and reduces user trust. Three variations of a confirmation prompt after saving a setting: (1) 'Done.' — use for rapid, low-stakes confirmations during a multi-step flow. (2) 'Got it. Your alarm is set for 7am.' — use when the user benefits from hearing the confirmed value. (3) 'All set. I will remind you at 7am tomorrow morning.' — use at the end of a conversation when fuller confirmation improves closure. Variation is selected based on context (conversation stage, importance of confirmation, user's interaction pace), not randomly.

rubric.key: Explains why variation prevents robotic feel
Context for when each variation is appropriate

major_assignments.title: Complete VUI Application Design
major_assignments.description: Design a complete voice user interface application including persona, dialog flows, error handling, and prompt scripts. Build a functional prototype in Voiceflow and test with at least 3 users.
objectives: Create a VUI persona aligned with the application domain and target users
Design comprehensive dialog flows with intent models and error handling
Write all prompts with variations for natural conversation
Prototype and test the voice experience with real users
deliverables: VUI persona document: personality, vocabulary, tone guidelines
Intent model with entities and 10+ sample utterances per intent
Dialog flow diagrams for 3+ user journeys including error paths
Complete prompt script with 3+ variations per prompt type
Voiceflow prototype and usability test report (3+ users)

criteria: Persona aligns with brand and user expectations
Vocabulary and tone are consistent throughout
Persona handles errors with appropriate personality
Intent model covers key user goals comprehensively
Slot filling and context management handle multi-turn conversations
Error handling follows escalation ladder pattern
Edge cases and unexpected inputs addressed
Prompts are concise and follow Grice's maxims
Variations provided for all prompt types
Confirmation prompts are appropriate for context
Error prompts guide rather than blame
Voiceflow prototype is functional and testable
Findings documented with specific interaction breakdowns
Iterations based on test findings are evident
Coherent VUI persona with consistent voice
Comprehensive dialog design with robust error handling

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on VUI design principles, dialog modeling, and prompt writing

vui_labs.description: Weekly labs designing dialog flows and building voice prototypes

vui_application_project.description: Complete VUI application with persona, dialog design, prototype, and testing

voice_critiques.description: Weekly analysis of commercial voice interfaces

final_presentation.description: Live demonstration and presentation of VUI application

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions involve voice prototyping and Wizard of Oz exercises that require participation. Mandatory attendance for all labs.
course_policies.late_submissions: Dialog design phases build sequentially. Late submissions penalized 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Wizard of Oz exercises are collaborative. All design deliverables and prototypes must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All dialog designs and prompts must be original. Using commercial VUI scripts or templates is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Voiceflow account, voice assistant device or smartphone with voice assistant, audio recording software, and a quiet space for voice testing.
